threats.title: Missing Authorization Check in updateOrder Allows Order Hijacking
threats.hypothesis_description: The updateOrder function allows any user with a valid position to modify the parameters of any active order, enabling attackers to hijack orders and steal collateral.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function retrieves an order from storage but fails to verify that the order belongs to the caller's position, unlike cancelOrder which correctly implements this check.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Missing ownership check on order struct

preconditions: Attacker has a valid margin position

initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No check linking the order to the caller's position

analysis_questions: Can an attacker modify the tokenOut to a worthless token?
Can the limitPrice be set to extract maximum value during fill?
validation_requirements: Test modifying another user's order
Test filling the modified order to extract collateral

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker modifies victim's order to have a highly unfavorable limit price and a worthless tokenOut, then fills the order to steal the victim's collateral.
prerequisites: Active margin orders in the system

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete loss of collateral for users with active orders
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users utilizing stop-loss or take-profit orders

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for cross-user order modification

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Minimal (enough to open a dummy position)
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Single transaction or two sequential transactions
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible on-chain via UpdateOrder events with mismatched posIds

threats.title: Swapped Return Values in _calculateFillOrderInfo Breaks Order Execution
threats.hypothesis_description: The return values of _calculateRepaySize are destructured in the wrong order in _calculateFillOrderInfo, causing the protocol to confuse debt shares with token amounts.
threats.investigation_rationale: _calculateRepaySize returns (repayAmt, repayShares), but the calling function destructures it as (repayShares, repayAmt). As interest accrues and the exchange rate diverges from 1:1, this will cause severe accounting errors and transaction reverts.
preconditions: Interest has accrued such that 1 share != 1 token

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: repayShares receives the token amount, repayAmt receives the share count

analysis_questions: Does this cause a permanent DoS on order filling?
Can this be exploited for profit if share price < 1?
validation_requirements: Simulate order fill after significant interest accrual
Verify token transfer amounts vs debt repayment amounts

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: When an order is filled, the executor pays the wrong amount of tokens, and the protocol attempts to repay the wrong number of shares, leading to reverts or incorrect debt reduction.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Orders cannot be filled, or incorrect amounts are transferred causing loss of funds

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for order filling after interest accrual

threats.title: Memory-Only Update in fillOrder Fails to Cancel Orders
threats.hypothesis_description: When fillOrder attempts to auto-cancel an order due to zero collateral, it updates a memory copy of the order struct instead of the storage struct, leaving the order active.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code uses 'Order memory order = __orders[_orderId];' and then sets 'order.status = OrderStatus.Cancelled;'. This does not modify the contract's state.

proof_of_signal.pattern: State update on memory struct instead of storage

preconditions: The position's collateral is reduced to 0

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: order.status update is lost when function returns

analysis_questions: Can the order be filled later if collateral is re-added?
Does this allow repeated emission of CancelOrder events?
validation_requirements: Call fillOrder on a zero-collateral position, verify status remains Active
Add collateral and successfully fill the 'cancelled' order

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: User's position is liquidated or reduced to 0 collateral. The order appears cancelled via events but remains active. User adds collateral later, and the old order unexpectedly executes.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Unexpected order execution leading to loss of newly added collateral
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users who reuse positions after collateral depletion

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Multiple CancelOrder events for the same order ID

threats.title: Strict Equality Check in coreCallback Enables DoS via Dust Transfers
threats.hypothesis_description: The coreCallback function uses a strict equality check for token balances during CloseExactOut swaps, allowing attackers to permanently DoS position reductions by sending 1 wei of the output token to the hook.
threats.investigation_rationale: The requirement `IERC20(swapInfo.tokenOut).balanceOf(address(this)) == swapInfo.amtOut` will fail if the contract holds any pre-existing balance of tokenOut.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Strict equality check on token balance

preconditions: User attempts to reduce position with a CloseExactOut swap

initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should use >= or track balance delta

analysis_questions: Can anyone send tokens to the hook contract?
Does this completely block reducePos functionality?
validation_requirements: Send 1 wei of token to the hook
Attempt to call reducePos and verify it reverts

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker front-runs a reducePos transaction by transferring 1 wei of the expected output token to the MarginTradingHook, causing the strict equality check to fail and reverting the victim's transaction.
prerequisites: Attacker monitors mempool for reducePos calls

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Inability to close or reduce margin positions, potentially leading to liquidation
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires admin intervention or contract upgrade

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests with pre-existing token balances in the hook

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Failed reducePos transactions accompanied by small token transfers

threats.title: Unprotected Native Token Sweep in MoneyMarketHook
threats.hypothesis_description: The execute function in MoneyMarketHook allows any caller to sweep all WNATIVE and ETH held by the contract by setting returnNative to true.
threats.investigation_rationale: If a user overpays msg.value during an operation (e.g., repaying less than the sent amount), the excess remains in the contract. Any subsequent caller can extract these funds.
preconditions: Contract holds residual WNATIVE or ETH from a previous transaction

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Balance sweep without tracking user deposits
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No accounting for individual user's ETH balance

analysis_questions: Under what conditions does the hook retain user funds?
Can MEV bots systematically extract these leftovers?
validation_requirements: Simulate a transaction that leaves ETH in the contract
Call execute with empty params and returnNative=true to sweep funds

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A user calls execute with msg.value = 1 ETH but only repays 0.8 ETH. 0.2 ETH remains in the hook. An MEV bot immediately calls execute with returnNative=true to steal the 0.2 ETH.
prerequisites: User error or imprecise calculation leaving funds in contract
threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for leftover fund extraction

threats.title: Multiple Orders Can Over-Commit Position Collateral
threats.hypothesis_description: The MarginTradingHook allows users to create multiple active orders whose combined collateral amounts exceed the total collateral of the position, leading to unexpected scaling during execution.
threats.investigation_rationale: _createOrder only checks if the individual order's collAmt is <= the position's current collateral. It does not aggregate the collAmt of all active orders.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Missing aggregate check across multiple orders

preconditions: User creates multiple orders on the same position

initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should sum collAmt of all active orders

analysis_questions: How does _calculateRepaySize handle insufficient collateral?
Does this allow users to manipulate the effective limit price?
validation_requirements: Create two orders that each commit 100% of collateral
Fill both orders sequentially and analyze the financial outcome

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A user creates two take-profit orders for 100% of their collateral. When the first fills, collateral is depleted. When the second fills, _calculateRepaySize scales the repayment proportionally, resulting in a different execution profile than intended.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Suboptimal execution prices for users, potential loss of value

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for multiple order execution on the same collateral

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Orders filling with scaled-down collateral amounts

threats.title: Default Price Deviation Threshold Causes Oracle DoS
threats.hypothesis_description: If a secondary oracle source is configured but the max price deviation threshold is left at its default (0), the oracle will always revert when fetching prices.
threats.investigation_rationale: The deviation check `(maxPrice_e36 * ONE_E18) / minPrice_e36 <= maxPriceDeviations_e18[_token]` evaluates to `X <= 0`, which is always false since prices are positive.
deployment_assumptions: maxPriceDeviations_e18 is not explicitly set for the token

proof_of_signal.pattern: Division by zero or impossible condition with default config

preconditions: Both primary and secondary sources return valid prices

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: maxPriceDeviations_e18 defaults to 0, making the check impossible to pass
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should enforce a minimum deviation threshold or skip check if 0

analysis_questions: Are there any deployment scripts that set secondary sources without setting deviations?
Does this block all core protocol functions?
validation_requirements: Deploy oracle with secondary source and 0 deviation
Call getPrice_e36 and verify it reverts

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Admin adds a secondary oracle source for redundancy but forgets to set the deviation threshold. The oracle immediately starts reverting, halting all borrows, liquidations, and margin trades for that token.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete protocol halt for affected tokens
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users interacting with the affected token
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Admin must set the deviation threshold

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for default configuration edge cases

threats.title: LP Price Manipulation via Flash Swaps When kLast is Zero
threats.hypothesis_description: When an AMM pair has the fee switch turned off (kLast == 0), the LP token price calculation relies on spot reserves, which can be manipulated via flash loans to inflate collateral value.
threats.investigation_rationale: The formula `2 * sqrt(r0 * p0) * sqrt(r1 * p1) / totalSupply` is susceptible to manipulation because swapping a large amount of tokens increases the product `r0 * r1` due to swap fees, artificially inflating the LP token price.
deployment_assumptions: Moe Pair has fee switch turned off (kLast == 0)

proof_of_signal.pattern: Spot reserve usage in price calculation

preconditions: Attacker has access to a large flash loan

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: r0 and r1 can be manipulated within a single block
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should use TWAP or kLast exclusively

analysis_questions: How much does the price inflate per swap?
Is the inflation sufficient to overcome the collateral factor and borrow fees?
validation_requirements: Simulate a massive flash loan swap on the pair
Calculate the resulting LP price increase
Attempt to borrow against the inflated collateral

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker flash loans a massive amount of token0, swaps it in the pair to inflate reserves via fees, wraps LP tokens, borrows assets against the inflated LP collateral, reverse swaps to repay the flash loan, and defaults on the debt.
prerequisites: Large liquidity available for flash loan

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Protocol insolvency due to undercollateralized bad debt
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Lenders in the borrowed asset pool

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing flash loan manipulation tests for LP pricing

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Large swaps followed by borrows and reverse swaps

threats.title: Zero Liquidation Incentive for Completely Underwater Positions
threats.hypothesis_description: If a position's health factor drops to exactly 0, the liquidation incentive multiplier evaluates to 0, preventing liquidators from receiving any collateral and leaving bad debt in the protocol.
threats.investigation_rationale: The calculator explicitly returns 0 if health is 0. Combined with the core logic that bypasses the post-liquidation health check for health == 0, liquidators would repay debt but receive 0 collateral.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Zero return value for edge case

initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should return maximum incentive for completely underwater positions

analysis_questions: How likely is a position to reach exactly 0 health?
Can an attacker intentionally drive health to 0 to prevent liquidation?
validation_requirements: Create a position with 0 health
Attempt to liquidate and verify 0 collateral is returned

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A position's collateral value drops to 0 (e.g., token price crashes to 0 or oracle is misconfigured). The position cannot be liquidated because the incentive is 0, leaving the debt permanently unbacked.
prerequisites: Extreme market conditions or oracle failure

threats.title: Incompatibility with Fee-on-Transfer Tokens Prevents Debt Repayment
threats.hypothesis_description: The LendingPool's repay function strictly checks that the increase in token balance matches the calculated repayment amount, which fails for fee-on-transfer tokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: The check `_require(amt <= IERC20(underlyingToken).balanceOf(address(this)) - _cash)` assumes the contract receives exactly `amt` tokens. For FOT tokens, the received amount is less, causing the transaction to revert.
deployment_assumptions: A fee-on-transfer token is whitelisted as a borrowable asset
preconditions: Underlying token has a transfer fee

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Fails to account for transfer fees
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should calculate actual received amount via balance before/after

analysis_questions: Are FOT tokens intended to be supported?
Does this trap user debt permanently?
validation_requirements: Deploy pool with FOT token
Borrow and attempt to repay, verify revert

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A user borrows a FOT token. When they attempt to repay, the transaction reverts because the pool receives less than the expected amount. The user's debt continues to grow, eventually leading to liquidation.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Users cannot repay debt, leading to unfair liquidations
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires admin to change token config or upgrade contract

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Failed repay transactions for specific pools

threats.title: Flashloan Fee Bypass via Atomic Borrow and Repay
threats.hypothesis_description: Users can bypass intended flashloan fees by using the multicall feature to atomically borrow and repay assets in the same block, accruing zero interest.
threats.investigation_rationale: Interest accrual uses `block.timestamp != _lastAccruedTime`. If borrow and repay occur in the same block, timeDelta is 0, resulting in 0 interest. This allows free flashloans, bypassing any fees that might be intended for the `flash` function.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Timestamp-based interest accrual allows zero-fee atomic borrow/repay

preconditions: User executes borrow and repay in the same transaction via multicall

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: No minimum fee or interest for same-block borrows
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should charge a minimum borrow fee or origination fee

analysis_questions: Is the flash function intended to charge a fee?
Does bypassing flash fees harm protocol revenue?
validation_requirements: Execute multicall with borrow and repay
Verify 0 interest is charged

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: An MEV bot needs a flashloan but wants to avoid fees. Instead of calling `flash`, it uses `multicall` to borrow, execute its arbitrage, and repay. Since it all happens in one block, no interest is charged.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss of potential protocol revenue from flashloans

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for same-block borrow/repay economics

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Multicalls containing both borrow and repay for the same pool
